course_info.title: Educational Technology Practicum
course_info.meeting_schedule: Friday - 1.5 hours seminar + 10 hours per week field placement

learning_objectives: Understand the professional expectations of the practicum placement
Develop practicum learning goals aligned to AECT/ISTE standards
Establish a professional relationship with the site supervisor
readings: AECT Standards for Academic Programs in Educational Technology

lecture_notes.introduction: The practicum is your transition from student to professional. You will spend 10 hours per week at a real educational technology site — a school district, corporate training department, university center for teaching and learning, ed tech company, or nonprofit — applying your skills under professional supervision.
key_concepts: Practicum site types: K-12 districts, higher education, corporate L&D, ed tech companies, nonprofits
Professional expectations: punctuality, dress code, confidentiality, communication norms
Learning goals: 3-5 specific, measurable goals aligned to professional standards
Supervisor relationship: regular meetings, feedback, professional mentoring
Documentation: time logs, reflective journals, supervisor evaluations
lecture_notes.demonstration: Goal-setting workshop: draft three practicum learning goals using SMART format, each aligned to a specific AECT or ISTE standard, and receive peer feedback

review.value: You represent the university and your profession. Treat the practicum as a job: be reliable, take initiative, ask questions, accept feedback gracefully, and maintain confidentiality about student/employee data.

Goals should stretch you beyond your comfort zone. If you are strong in instructional design but weak in stakeholder communication, set a goal around presenting to leadership or facilitating faculty workshops.

class_schedule.topic: Professional Identity and the Ed Tech Career Landscape
learning_objectives: Map the educational technology career landscape
Identify career paths and professional development opportunities

lecture_notes.introduction: The practicum is also a career exploration opportunity. Educational technology professionals work in diverse settings — understanding the career landscape helps you make informed decisions about your path.
key_concepts: Career paths: instructional designer, learning technologist, LMS administrator, training manager, ed tech consultant, researcher
Settings: K-12, higher education, corporate, government, nonprofit, ed tech industry
Professional organizations: AECT, ISTE, ATD, EDUCAUSE, OLC
Professional portfolio: curating evidence of your competencies
Networking: LinkedIn, conferences, professional communities
lecture_notes.demonstration: Portfolio planning: identify 5 artifacts from your coursework that demonstrate different professional competencies and draft brief reflection statements for each
setup_requirements: Portfolio platform options (WordPress, Google Sites, or similar)

review.value: A portfolio is not a resume — it is evidence. Each artifact should demonstrate a specific competency with a reflection explaining what you did, why, and what you learned.
Use your practicum network to conduct 2-3 informational interviews with professionals in roles you find interesting. This is the most effective career exploration strategy.

learning_objectives: Analyze organizational culture and its impact on technology adoption
Navigate professional relationships with diverse stakeholders
Apply strategies for influencing without authority
readings: Bolman, L. & Deal, T. - Reframing Organizations Ch. 1 (four frames)
Practical guide to professional communication in ed tech settings

lecture_notes.introduction: The practicum will expose you to organizational dynamics that courses cannot simulate. Understanding how organizations really work — the informal power structures, political dynamics, and cultural norms — is as important as your technical skills.
key_concepts: Organizational culture: artifacts, espoused values, basic assumptions (Schein)
Four frames: structural, human resource, political, symbolic (Bolman & Deal)
Influencing without authority: building credibility, demonstrating value, understanding stakeholder motivations
Managing up: communicating with supervisors and administrators effectively
Professional conflict: disagreeing respectfully, advocating for students and best practices
lecture_notes.demonstration: Organizational analysis: use the four frames to analyze your practicum site and identify the primary frame through which your supervisor operates

review.value: In most organizations, technology decisions are political: they involve competing interests, limited resources, and power dynamics. Understanding who controls the budget, who has veto power, and who influences whom is essential.

As a practicum student, you have expertise but not authority. Influence through: demonstrating quick wins, speaking the language of your audience (ROI for administrators, time savings for teachers), and building relationships before asking for change.

learning_objectives: Assess progress toward practicum learning goals
Identify challenges and develop strategies to address them
Refine professional skills based on supervisor and peer feedback
readings: No assigned readings — focus on reflection and adjustment

lecture_notes.introduction: At the midpoint of the practicum, it is time to honestly assess your progress, celebrate what is going well, and address what needs to change.
key_concepts: Goal progress assessment: on track, behind, or exceeding expectations
Challenge identification: technical challenges, relationship challenges, scope challenges
Feedback integration: using supervisor feedback to improve performance
Skill gaps: identifying areas where you need additional development
Adjusting goals: when and how to modify practicum objectives
lecture_notes.demonstration: Structured reflection: each student shares a success story and a challenge from the practicum, then receives problem-solving suggestions from peers

review.value: Feeling uncomfortable or challenged at your practicum site is normal and healthy. If everything feels easy, you are not being stretched enough. Seek out harder tasks.
Professional competence includes knowing when to ask for help. Struggling silently is not a sign of strength. Asking a supervisor for guidance shows good judgment and initiative.

class_schedule.topic: Professional Skills: Communication and Stakeholder Management
learning_objectives: Develop professional communication skills for diverse audiences
Practice presenting technical concepts to non-technical stakeholders
Apply project management skills in the practicum context
readings: Duarte, N. - Slide:ology Ch. 1-3 (adapted for professional presentations)
Professional email and meeting communication guides

lecture_notes.introduction: Technical skill alone does not make a successful educational technologist. Your ability to communicate clearly, manage stakeholders, and present professionally determines whether your ideas are adopted.
key_concepts: Audience analysis: adjusting message for teachers, administrators, IT, parents, students
Executive communication: one-page summaries, elevator pitches, data storytelling
Meeting management: agendas, notes, follow-up actions
Email professionalism: subject lines, structure, tone, when to email vs. meet
Handling pushback: responding to resistance constructively
lecture_notes.demonstration: Elevator pitch practice: deliver a 2-minute pitch for a practicum initiative to three different audiences (teacher, principal, IT director), adjusting message and emphasis for each

review.value: Administrators do not read 10-page reports. They read the executive summary. Every recommendation you make should be supported by a one-page summary with: problem, recommendation, evidence, cost, and next steps.

Teachers want to know: will this save me time? Administrators want to know: what does it cost and what is the evidence? IT wants to know: is it secure, scalable, and supportable? Speak their language.

learning_objectives: Complete the practicum with professional documentation and handoff
Finalize the professional portfolio with practicum artifacts
Articulate your professional identity and career direction

lecture_notes.introduction: The practicum ends, but your professional journey continues. This session focuses on leaving your site well, documenting your work, and transitioning from student to professional.
key_concepts: Professional exit: documentation, handoff, thank-you communications
Portfolio completion: selecting and reflecting on practicum artifacts
Lessons learned: what you would do differently with the benefit of experience
Career next steps: job search, networking, continued professional development
Professional identity statement: who you are as an ed tech professional
lecture_notes.demonstration: Professional identity presentation: each student delivers a 3-minute professional identity statement articulating their values, strengths, and career direction

review.value: Leave your practicum site better than you found it. Document your work so someone can continue it. Thank your supervisor and colleagues. Maintain the relationship — your practicum supervisor is a professional reference and potential future colleague.

The field of educational technology changes constantly. Your degree is a foundation, not a destination. Commit to continuous learning through professional organizations, conferences, webinars, and staying current with research.

major_assignments.title: Practicum Portfolio and Reflective Report
major_assignments.description: Compile a professional portfolio documenting your practicum experience. The portfolio must include: practicum learning goals with evidence of achievement, 5+ artifacts demonstrating professional competencies, reflective narratives for each artifact, supervisor evaluation, and a summative reflective essay connecting practicum experiences to your professional identity and career goals.
objectives: Document professional competency development through curated artifacts
Reflect critically on practicum experiences and professional growth
Receive and integrate supervisor evaluation feedback
Articulate a professional identity grounded in experience
deliverables: Professional portfolio (digital) with 5+ artifacts and reflections

criteria: Clear connection between artifacts and AECT/ISTE standards
Reflections demonstrate critical thinking, not just description
Identifies both successes and areas for growth
Connects experiences to theoretical foundations from coursework
Evidence of professional conduct and initiative
Demonstrated ability to work in a professional setting
Thoughtful synthesis of the practicum experience
Honest assessment of strengths and areas for continued development
Comprehensive portfolio with diverse, high-quality artifacts
Deep reflective practice connecting experience to theory

practicum_performance.description: Supervisor evaluations of professional conduct, initiative, and competence

portfolio.description: Professional portfolio with artifacts and reflections

reflective_essays.description: Weekly reflective journal entries and summative reflective essay

seminar_participation.description: Active participation in weekly seminar discussions and peer consultation

professionalism.description: Time log completion, communication with supervisor, and professional conduct

course_policies.attendance: Weekly seminar attendance is mandatory. Practicum site attendance must meet the minimum 10 hours per week (150 total hours). Absences from the practicum site must be communicated to both the site supervisor and the university instructor.
course_policies.late_submissions: Reflective journal entries are due weekly. Late entries lose 10% per day. The final portfolio deadline is firm.
course_policies.collaboration: Peer consultation during seminars is expected. All reflective writing and portfolio content must be individually authored.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All portfolio artifacts must be your own work or properly attributed. Practicum site confidentiality must be maintained. Do not include identifiable student or employee data without permission.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Portfolio platform (WordPress, Google Sites, or similar). Computer for documentation and reflection writing.
